content: Load estimation converts DAU to QPS: 100M DAU with each user making 10 requests/day = 1B requests/day = ~11,574 QPS average. Peak traffic is typically 2-3x average, so ~30,000 QPS peak. Rule of thumb: 1M DAU ~ 12 QPS average, 36 QPS peak.

Database selection heuristics: SQL (PostgreSQL, MySQL) for relational data with ACID transactions and complex joins. NoSQL document stores (MongoDB) for flexible schemas and horizontal scaling. Key-value stores (Redis, DynamoDB) for low-latency lookups. Time-series databases (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) for metrics with time-based queries and automatic downsampling.

Caching operates in layers: L1 in-process cache (Python dict, guava) with ~1ms latency and process-local scope, L2 distributed cache (Redis, Memcached) with ~1-5ms latency shared across instances, L3 CDN (CloudFront, Cloudflare) at edge locations with ~10-50ms for static content. Cache hit rates above 90% typically reduce database load by 10x.

Consistent hashing maps both servers and keys to positions on a hash ring. When a server is added/removed, only K/N keys need remapping (K=total keys, N=servers). Virtual nodes (100-200 per physical server) improve load balance by distributing each server to multiple ring positions, reducing variance from O(log N) to O(1/sqrt(V*N)).

Message queue delivery semantics: at-most-once (fire and forget, possible message loss), at-least-once (acknowledge after processing, possible duplicates requiring idempotent consumers), exactly-once (transactional outbox + idempotent consumer, highest complexity). Kafka achieves exactly-once via idempotent producers + transactional APIs.

CAP theorem states a distributed system can guarantee at most 2 of 3: Consistency (every read returns the latest write), Availability (every request receives a response), Partition tolerance (system operates despite network splits). Since partitions are inevitable, the real choice is CP (reject requests during partition) vs AP (serve stale data during partition).

Eventual consistency patterns: Last-Write-Wins (LWW) uses timestamps to resolve conflicts but can lose writes. Vector clocks track causal ordering across replicas to detect concurrent writes. CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) are data structures that always converge: G-Counter, PN-Counter, OR-Set, LWW-Register.

Back-of-envelope calculations: 1M users * 1KB profile = 1GB storage. 1 photo (500KB) * 10M uploads/day = 5TB/day. 1 server handles ~10K concurrent TCP connections (C10K problem solved). SSD random read: 100 microseconds. Network round-trip within datacenter: 0.5ms. Cross-continent: 150ms.

Horizontal scaling (adding more machines) vs vertical scaling (bigger machine): horizontal provides near-linear throughput scaling and fault tolerance but adds distributed system complexity (consistency, coordination). Vertical scaling is simpler but has hardware limits (~256 cores, 24TB RAM for high-end servers in 2026) and is a single point of failure.

Read/write ratio optimization: for 90/10 read-heavy workloads, use read replicas (3-5 replicas per primary), caching layers, and materialized views. For 50/50 balanced workloads, use write-behind caching and sharding. For write-heavy workloads (logging, metrics), use append-only stores (Kafka, LSM-tree databases like RocksDB/Cassandra).

Database sharding strategies: range-based (shard by date range or ID range, risk of hotspots), hash-based (consistent hashing on shard key, even distribution but no range queries), directory-based (lookup table maps keys to shards, flexible but adds a bottleneck). Choose shard key carefully: high cardinality, even distribution, and locality for common queries.

The write-ahead log (WAL) ensures durability by writing changes to a sequential log before applying them to the data file. On crash, uncommitted changes are replayed from the WAL. PostgreSQL, SQLite (WAL mode), and all LSM-tree databases use this pattern. WAL writes are sequential, making them 100x faster than random writes on spinning disks.

API design principles: use nouns not verbs in URLs (GET /users/123 not GET /getUser?id=123), use HTTP methods correctly (GET=read, POST=create, PUT=full update, PATCH=partial update, DELETE=remove), version APIs (v1/v2 in URL or Accept header), paginate collections (cursor-based for real-time data, offset-based for static).

Circuit breaker pattern prevents cascading failures: Closed state (requests pass through, track failures), Open state (requests fail immediately after failure threshold, e.g., 5 failures in 60 seconds), Half-Open state (after timeout, allow one test request). If test succeeds, return to Closed; if fails, return to Open. Libraries: resilience4j, pybreaker, tenacity.

Event sourcing stores state changes as an immutable sequence of events rather than mutable current state. The current state is derived by replaying events. Benefits: complete audit trail, temporal queries, event replay for debugging. Drawbacks: eventual consistency, complex read models (CQRS), growing event store requiring snapshotting.

Bloom filters are space-efficient probabilistic data structures that test set membership. They can return false positives but never false negatives. With m bits and k hash functions for n elements, false positive rate is approximately (1 - e^(-kn/m))^k. Optimal k = (m/n) * ln(2). Used in databases to skip disk reads for non-existent keys.

Leader election in distributed systems uses algorithms like Raft (majority quorum, heartbeat-based), Bully (highest ID wins), or ZooKeeper's ZAB protocol. Raft requires majority (N/2 + 1) of nodes for leader election and log replication, so a 5-node cluster tolerates 2 failures while maintaining availability.

Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) cache content at edge locations (200+ PoPs globally). Cache invalidation strategies: TTL-based (set max-age in Cache-Control header), purge API (instant invalidation), versioned URLs (append hash to filename for immutable caching). Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable for static assets with content hashes.

Database indexing: B-tree indexes support equality and range queries with O(log N) lookup. Hash indexes support only equality with O(1) lookup. Composite indexes follow the leftmost prefix rule: index on (a, b, c) supports queries on (a), (a, b), and (a, b, c) but not (b) or (c) alone. Covering indexes include all queried columns, eliminating table lookups.

Microservices communication: synchronous (REST, gRPC with protobuf for 10x smaller payloads and code generation) vs asynchronous (message queues for decoupling, event streaming for real-time). Service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) provides mTLS, load balancing, circuit breaking, and observability without application code changes.

Idempotency in distributed systems prevents duplicate processing. Use idempotency keys: client generates a unique request ID, server checks if that ID was already processed before executing. Store processed IDs in Redis with TTL (24 hours). For payments, idempotency is critical to prevent double-charging.

Rate limiting at scale uses distributed counters in Redis. Fixed window: INCR key with EXPIRE. Sliding window log: ZADD with timestamp scores, ZRANGEBYSCORE to count. Sliding window counter: weighted average of current and previous fixed windows. Token bucket with Redis: MULTI/EXEC atomic decrement and refill.

Data replication strategies: single-leader (one write node, multiple read replicas, simple but write bottleneck), multi-leader (multiple write nodes, conflict resolution needed, useful for multi-datacenter), leaderless (quorum reads/writes with W + R > N, Dynamo-style, highest availability). Replication lag in single-leader typically ranges from milliseconds to seconds.

Observability comprises three pillars: metrics (aggregated numerical measurements via Prometheus/Grafana), logs (structured event records via ELK/Loki), and traces (distributed request flow via Jaeger/Zipkin). OpenTelemetry provides a unified SDK for all three. Golden signals to monitor: latency, traffic, errors, saturation.

Load balancing algorithms: round-robin (simple, ignores server health), weighted round-robin (accounts for server capacity), least connections (routes to least busy server), consistent hashing (session affinity without sticky sessions), random with two choices (power of two choices: pick 2 random servers, choose less loaded one, near-optimal distribution).
